perturbation.original_text: 1.02 "COMPETE" OR "COMPETING" shall mean with respect to the Business: (i) the design, manufacture, marketing, sale, leasing or distribution of health care products of the types or performing the functions of the products designed, manufactured, marketed, sold, leased and distributed by Seller through the Division as of the date of this Agreement; (ii) hiring, soliciting or attempting to hire or solicit any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date either on Seller's behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity; or (iii) entering into or attempting to enter into any business substantially similar to the Business, either alone or with any individual, partnership, corporation or association; provided, however, that Competing shall not mean or include the ownership, disposition or use of Excluded Assets.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.02 "COMPETE" OR "COMPETING" shall mean with respect to the Business: (i) the design, manufacture, marketing, sale, leasing or distribution of health care products of the types or performing the functions of the products designed, manufactured, marketed, sold, leased and distributed by Seller through the Division as of the date of this Agreement; (ii) hiring, soliciting or attempting to hire or solicit any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date either on Seller's behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity; or (iii) entering into or attempting to enter into any business substantially similar to the Business, either alone or with any individual, partnership, corporation or association; provided, however, that Competing shall not mean or include the ownership, disposition or use of Excluded Assets. **Furthermore, for the purposes of this agreement, 'Competing' shall only be considered applicable if the competing business generates revenue exceeding $10,000,000 annually.**
perturbation.explanation: The added clause introduces a financial threshold for what constitutes 'Competing,' potentially conflicting with the established definition and introducing ambiguity. New York law does not automatically exclude competition based on revenue thresholds alone.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's General Business Law § 340 prohibits contracts or agreements that restrain competition. By adding a revenue threshold, the contract implies that competition below that threshold is permissible, even if it still technically violates the original definition of 'competing'. This creates a potential conflict and uncertainty regarding enforcement if the Seller engages in activities that, by the original definition, are competing, but do not meet the revenue threshold. The state's definition is more comprehensive than the contract's newly defined 'Competing.'
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a revenue threshold of $10,000,000 for defining 'Competing,' implying that competition below this threshold is permissible. The Law Explanation states that New York General Business Law § 340 prohibits contracts that restrain competition. The added clause creates a potential conflict because the state's definition is more comprehensive than the contract's newly defined 'Competing.' If the seller engages in activities that, by the original definition, are competing, but do not meet the revenue threshold, it creates uncertainty and contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed. (b) Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement **"trade secrets" means only patentable information that is kept secret.** Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed. (b) Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowly defining "trade secrets" as only patentable information, the contract contradicts the broader legal definition of trade secrets, which includes a wide range of confidential business information that provides a competitive edge. This creates a loophole where the seller could disclose valuable, non-patentable trade secrets without violating the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets much more broadly than just patentable information. NY CPLR § 3101(a)(4) establishes the scope of discovery in litigation, which is extremely relevant to the concept of trade secrets. By limiting the definition of trade secrets to only patentable information, the contract is inconsistent with the statute and creates a situation where information that should be protected as a trade secret is not.

Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the substance of the facts and opinions on which each expert is expected to testify, the qualifications of each expert witness and a summary of the grounds for each expert's opinion. However, where a party for good cause shown retains an expert an insufficient period of time before the commencement of trial to give appropriate notice thereof, the party shall not thereupon be precluded from introducing the expert's testimony at the trial solely on grounds of noncompliance with this paragraph.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by narrowly defining 'trade secrets' as 'only patentable information that is kept secret.' This contradicts the broader legal understanding of trade secrets, which includes a wide range of confidential business information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this narrow definition allows the seller to disclose valuable, non-patentable trade secrets without violating the contract. The Law Explanation also points out that NY CPLR § 3101(a)(4) (as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2) establishes a scope of discovery relevant to trade secrets, and the contract's limitation is inconsistent with the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 broadly defines the scope of disclosure as 'full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action,' and narrowing trade secrets to only patentable information conflicts with this general scope, making information that should be protected as a trade secret potentially discoverable.

perturbation.original_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory. **However, this non-competition clause shall only be enforceable if Purchasers maintain a market share of at least 75% within the Territory.**
perturbation.explanation: The added condition regarding market share introduces an external factor for the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. This creates uncertainty and potentially conflicts with the established understanding of non-compete agreements under New York law, which focuses on reasonableness in scope, duration, and geographic area, not market share.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Law Regarding Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 340 addresses agreements for monopoly or in restraint of trade. New York courts evaluate non-compete agreements based on reasonableness and whether they are necessary to protect legitimate business interests. Adding a condition related to maintaining a specific market share is not a typical consideration and can be argued as an unreasonable restraint if the non-compete is enforced even when the purchaser's market share falls below the stipulated amount. It may be viewed as anticompetitive.
perturbation.justification: The added condition to the non-compete clause, requiring the Purchasers to maintain a 75% market share for the clause to be enforceable, introduces an element that deviates from the established legal standards for evaluating non-compete agreements in New York. According to the Law Explanation, New York courts assess non-compete agreements based on their reasonableness and necessity to protect legitimate business interests, not on market share. The condition makes enforceability contingent on a factor unrelated to the seller's conduct or the protection of the purchaser's legitimate business interests, potentially conflicting with New York General Business Law § 340 concerning agreements in restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity. **However, this clause shall not apply to employees who voluntarily resign from the Purchasers for reasons unrelated to any actions by the Seller.**
perturbation.explanation: While seemingly reasonable, the addition of 'employees who voluntarily resign from the Purchasers for reasons unrelated to any actions by the Seller' introduces a subjective and potentially difficult-to-prove condition. This can be read to contradict the intent of the non-solicitation agreement, weakening its enforceability because demonstrating the reason an employee leaves is difficult and courts dislike overbroad agreements that limit employee mobility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Law Regarding Employee Freedom of Movement
perturbation.law_citation: Reed, Roberts Assoc., Inc. v. Strauman, 40 N.Y.2d 303 (1976)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific statutory citation, the New York Court of Appeals case *Reed, Roberts Assoc., Inc. v. Strauman* establishes the legal precedent for the limitations of non-solicitation agreements. New York courts are wary of agreements that unduly restrict employee mobility. The added clause creates a loophole and places a heavy burden on the purchaser to prove that a voluntary resignation was *not* independent of the seller's influence. If the clause is deemed too vague or broad, it can render the entire provision unenforceable under established case law, going against the intent to restrict solicitation. The change might make it harder to prove inducement since demonstrating the *reason* for resignation becomes key.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 'loophole' allowing employees to voluntarily resign for reasons unrelated to the seller's actions. The Law Explanation highlights that New York courts are wary of agreements that unduly restrict employee mobility, and the added clause creates a burden on the purchaser to prove that a voluntary resignation was *not* independent of the seller's influence. The perturbation explanation also notes this weakens enforceability. The absence of explicit law snippets doesn't negate the contradiction; the LLM-provided reasoning, coupled with the subjective condition added to the contract (voluntary resignation for unrelated reasons), sufficiently establishes that the change makes the non-solicitation agreement harder to enforce, thus contradicting its intent.
